news_summary: Advances in treatments using omega-3, immune cell therapy, and melatonin offer new hope for diabetes, cirrhosis, and DNA repair, while DR Congo marks its first recovery from Ebola.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans les traitements à base d'oméga-3, la thérapie cellulaire immunitaire et la mélatonine offrent un nouvel espoir pour le diabète, la cirrhose et la réparation de l'ADN, tandis que la République démocratique du Congo célèbre sa première guérison du virus Ebola.
news_summary_es: Los avances en los tratamientos con omega-3, la terapia con células inmunitarias y la melatonina ofrecen nuevas esperanzas para la diabetes, la cirrosis y la reparación del ADN, mientras que la República Democrática del Congo celebra su primera recuperación del ébola.

articles.title: Omega-3 fish oil shows promise against type 2 diabetes
articles.summary: A new study suggests fish oil may help reduce insulin resistance even in people who aren't obese. In diabetic rats, omega-3 supplementation improved blood sugar levels, cholesterol, and inflammation by shifting immune cells into a more anti-inflammatory mode.
articles.content:
Fish oil may have a surprising role in the fight against insulin resistance, especially in a form of type 2 diabetes that is often overlooked. A Brazilian study published in Nutrients found that omega-3 fatty acids from fish oil reduced glucose intolerance and weakened insulin resistance in rats that were not obese but showed a diabetes-like metabolic condition.

The work was funded by FAPESP and focused on Goto-Kakizaki rats, a well established animal model used to study non-obese type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is marked by high blood sugar that occurs when insulin, the hormone that helps move glucose from the blood into cells, does not work effectively.

Fish Oil and Insulin Resistance

Omega-3 supplements, including fish oil, are often used by people with cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes. However, scientists still know much less about how these fatty acids affect insulin resistance when obesity is not involved.

That question matters because obesity is one of the strongest risk factors for type 2 diabetes, but it is not the whole story. An estimated 10% to 20% of people with type 2 diabetes worldwide are not obese. For these patients, the biological roots of insulin resistance may differ from the better known obesity-linked pathways.

In the study, researchers gave the rats fish oil at a dose of 2 grams per kilogram of body weight (equivalent to 540 mg/g of eicosapentaenoic acid, or EPA, and 100 mg/g of docosahexaenoic acid, or DHA) three times weekly for eight weeks. By the end of the experiment, the treated animals showed lower insulin resistance, better blood sugar control, reduced inflammatory markers, and improvements in several lipid measures, including total cholesterol, LDL ("bad cholesterol") and triglycerides.

The results came from preclinical experiments, so they do not prove that fish oil will have the same effects in people. Still, the findings point to inflammation as a powerful target in non-obese diabetes and suggest that omega-3 fatty acids deserve closer study in this group.

A Shift in Immune Cells

"Our experiments involved Goto-Kakizaki [GK] rats, an animal model for non-obese type 2 diabetes. We found that insulin resistance can be reduced in these animals by modulating the inflammatory response so as to change the profile of defense cells [lymphocytes] from a pro-inflammatory state to an anti-inflammatory state. This process parallels the response of obese individuals with insulin resistance to omega-3 fatty acid supplementation," said Rui Curi, Director of Butantan Institute's Education Center, Professor of Interdisciplinary Graduate Studies in Health Sciences at Cruzeiro do Sul University (UNICSUL), and coordinator of the study.

Lymphocytes are white blood cells that help direct the adaptive immune response. When their behavior changes, the effects can spread through the immune system and influence other cells involved in inflammation.

"In previous studies, we observed alterations in both lymphocytes and macrophages [large white blood cells that often reside in adipose tissue and are part of the innate immune system, engulfing and destroying pathogens] in non-obese rats with insulin resistance. In such cases, these cells produce more pro-inflammatory cytokines, as is central in obese people with diabetes," Curi explained.

"The main aim of the study, therefore, was to find out whether supplementation with fish oil [rich in omega-3] could reverse specific alterations in lymphocytes that had been observed in previous research. Our findings increased our knowledge of the link between inflammation and insulin resistance in non-obese animals, confirming that this is a key factor in diabetes even in the absence of obesity," said Renata Gorjão, last author of the article, and Co-Director of UNICSUL's Program of Graduate Studies in Health Sciences.

Inflammation Without Obesity

The Nutrients study, conducted during the PhD candidacy of Tiago Bertola Lobato, was part of a broader FAPESP-supported project exploring how insulin resistance develops in non-obese animals.

Curi noted that obesity is a major diabetes risk factor, but not the only one. In people who develop diabetes without obesity, one leading hypothesis is that genetic factors may play an important role. In another study published in Cells, Curi, Gorjão, and colleagues investigated whether delayed intestinal transit might also contribute to insulin resistance in non-obese individuals.

"Most obese people have chronic low-level inflammation, which is known to affect the insulin signaling pathways. Adipose tissue, which is augmented in obesity, releases pro-inflammatory cytokines that affect the insulin signaling pathways, promoting insulin resistance. In the non-obese model, this impactful characteristic of adipose tissue is absent, but systemic inflammation is present," Curi said.

The group had previously shown systemic inflammation in non-obese GK rats with insulin resistance in a study published in the International Journal of Molecular Sciences.

Another paper from the same project reported that anti-inflammatory defenses appear to break down early in non-obese GK rats with insulin resistance. Lymph nodes (part of the immune system) from newly weaned 21-day-old GK pups already showed reduced markers of regulatory T-cells (Tregs, cells with anti-inflammatory characteristics). The researchers also detected other early inflammatory changes. That work was published in FEBS Letters, a journal of the Federation of European Biochemical Societies.

How Omega-3s May Help

The Nutrients study suggests that fish oil may work by moving immune activity away from a damaging inflammatory pattern and toward a more protective one.

"Fish oil supplementation reversed this pro-inflammatory profile, displaying a significant anti-inflammatory effect and reducing polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells [lymphocyte subtypes that perform crucial functions in inflammation], followed by a rise in the percentage of Tregs, which can inhibit the activation of pro-inflammatory lymphocytes. Thus the action of omega-3 fatty acids on lymphocytes, modulating them from a pro-inflammatory state to an anti-inflammatory state, may have triggered the reduction in insulin resistance in these animals," Lobato said.

That immune shift is important because insulin resistance is not only a problem of sugar metabolism. It is also deeply connected to inflammation. When inflammatory signals remain elevated, they can interfere with insulin signaling and make it harder for cells to respond to the hormone.

The study adds to a growing view of type 2 diabetes as a disease shaped by both metabolism and the immune system. In this case, fish oil appeared to improve blood sugar regulation not simply by changing fat levels, but by changing the inflammatory environment that helps drive insulin resistance.

What Later Studies Add

Since the Nutrients paper was published, related human research has continued to examine how omega-3 fatty acids may influence early diabetes risk and metabolic health.

A 2025 double blind randomized controlled trial in Food and Function tested fish oil supplementation in healthy middle aged and older adults. Over 12 weeks, the fish oil groups had dose related increases in serum EPA and DHA. The researchers also reported decreases in fasting insulin and the HOMA-IR index, a common marker of insulin resistance. Fasting blood glucose trended downward across groups, and several lipid related measures also improved.

Another 2024 analysis in Nutrition and Diabetes used modeling data from 161 patients with type 2 diabetes to explore the relationship between omega-3 levels and HbA1c, a longer term marker of blood sugar control. The authors reported a dose related association and proposed that omega-3 intake could be studied in a more individualized way, while also noting that the role of omega-3s in type 2 diabetes remains debated.

Together, these studies do not settle the question of whether fish oil should be used to manage diabetes. Human evidence remains mixed, and the Brazilian study was conducted in animals, not people. However, the newer findings are consistent with the idea that omega-3 fatty acids may affect insulin resistance and inflammation in ways worth testing more carefully.

More Research Still Needed

Despite the promising findings, the researchers stressed that the results should be interpreted cautiously. Animal studies are useful for uncovering biological mechanisms, but clinical trials are needed before scientists can know whether the same strategy works in people with non-obese type 2 diabetes.

"These studies involved well-established experimental models that mimic insulin resistance in non-obese individuals. Trials in humans are needed to estimate the ideal dose and the most indicated type of omega-3 fatty acid," Curi said.

For now, the study offers a compelling clue: in diabetes, body weight may not be the only driver of insulin resistance. Inflammation can play a central role even without obesity, and fish oil may help reveal how that hidden process can be changed.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific study showing that omega-3 fish oil supplementation may reduce insulin resistance and inflammation in a non-obese model of type 2 diabetes, highlighting a novel approach to understanding and potentially treating a substantial subset of diabetes patients. The findings have broad health implications, especially since 10-20% of type 2 diabetics are non-obese, and the study provides detailed context and potential mechanisms, making it a substantive and inspiring development in diabetes research.
articles.personality_title: Fish oil may reduce insulin resistance in non-obese type 2 diabetes, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Type 2 diabetes is often linked to obesity, but about 10% to 20% of people with this disease are not obese. Scientists want to understand why insulin resistance happens in these cases, as it may involve different biological processes, especially related to inflammation.

**What happened** – A Brazilian study tested omega-3 fish oil on non-obese diabetic rats called Goto-Kakizaki rats. The rats received fish oil three times a week for eight weeks. Researchers found that fish oil lowered insulin resistance, improved blood sugar levels, and reduced inflammation by changing certain immune cells from a harmful state to a protective one.

**Impact** – This study is important because it shows that fish oil might help people with type 2 diabetes who are not obese, a group often ignored in research. It highlights that inflammation, not just obesity, can cause insulin resistance. The study also explains how omega-3 fatty acids influence immune cells to reduce inflammation, which helps control blood sugar.

**What's next step** – Although the results are promising, they come from animal tests. Scientists say more research, especially clinical trials with people, is needed to find the right fish oil dose and confirm if it works the same way in humans with non-obese type 2 diabetes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Fish oil’s omega-3 fatty acids may reduce insulin resistance and inflammation in non-obese type 2 diabetes by shifting immune cells to a less harmful state, offering a new direction for future research.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’huile de poisson pourrait réduire la résistance à l’insuline dans le diabète de type 2 non obèse, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le diabète de type 2 est souvent associé à l’obésité, mais environ 10 à 20 % des personnes atteintes ne sont pas obèses. Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre pourquoi la résistance à l’insuline survient dans ces cas, car elle pourrait impliquer des processus biologiques différents, notamment liés à l’inflammation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude brésilienne a testé l’huile de poisson riche en oméga-3 sur des rats diabétiques non obèses appelés rats Goto-Kakizaki. Les rats ont reçu de l’huile de poisson trois fois par semaine pendant huit semaines. Les chercheurs ont constaté que l’huile réduisait la résistance à l’insuline, améliorait le contrôle du sucre sanguin et diminuait l’inflammation en transformant certains globules blancs d’un état nocif à un état protecteur.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle montre que l’huile de poisson pourrait aider les personnes atteintes de diabète de type 2 non obèses, un groupe souvent négligé. Elle souligne que l’inflammation, et non seulement l’obésité, peut causer la résistance à l’insuline. L’étude explique aussi comment les acides gras oméga-3 influencent les cellules immunitaires pour réduire l’inflammation, ce qui aide à contrôler le sucre sanguin.

**Prochaine étape** – Bien que les résultats soient prometteurs, ils proviennent d’expériences sur des animaux. Les scientifiques soulignent la nécessité de réaliser davantage d’études, notamment des essais cliniques sur des humains, pour déterminer la dose idéale d’huile de poisson et confirmer son effet chez les personnes atteintes de diabète non obèse.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les acides gras oméga-3 de l’huile de poisson pourraient réduire la résistance à l’insuline et l’inflammation dans le diabète de type 2 non obèse en modifiant l’état des cellules immunitaires, ouvrant une nouvelle voie pour la recherche.
articles.personality_title_es: El aceite de pescado podría reducir la resistencia a la insulina en diabetes tipo 2 no obesos, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La diabetes tipo 2 suele estar relacionada con la obesidad, pero entre el 10 % y el 20 % de las personas con esta enfermedad no son obesas. Los científicos quieren entender por qué ocurre la resistencia a la insulina en estos casos, ya que puede involucrar procesos biológicos diferentes, especialmente relacionados con la inflamación.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio brasileño probó el aceite de pescado rico en omega-3 en ratas diabéticas no obesas llamadas ratas Goto-Kakizaki. Las ratas recibieron aceite de pescado tres veces por semana durante ocho semanas. Los investigadores encontraron que el aceite redujo la resistencia a la insulina, mejoró el control del azúcar en sangre y disminuyó la inflamación al cambiar ciertas células inmunes de un estado dañino a uno protector.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque muestra que el aceite de pescado podría ayudar a personas con diabetes tipo 2 que no son obesas, un grupo que a menudo se ignora en la investigación. Destaca que la inflamación, y no solo la obesidad, puede causar resistencia a la insulina. El estudio también explica cómo los ácidos grasos omega-3 influyen en las células inmunes para reducir la inflamación, lo que ayuda a controlar el azúcar en sangre.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque los resultados son prometedores, provienen de pruebas en animales. Los científicos dicen que se necesitan más investigaciones, especialmente ensayos clínicos en personas, para encontrar la dosis adecuada de aceite de pescado y confirmar si funciona igual en humanos con diabetes tipo 2 no obesos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los ácidos grasos omega-3 del aceite de pescado pueden reducir la resistencia a la insulina y la inflamación en la diabetes tipo 2 no obesos al cambiar el estado de las células inmunes, ofreciendo una nueva dirección para futuras investigaciones.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a healthy, vibrant fish swimming gracefully among glowing, stylized omega-3 fatty acid molecules shaped like gentle waves, intertwined with soft, abstract representations of white blood cells shifting from fiery red to calming blue hues, symbolizing the transformation from inflammation to healing in a non-obese metabolic environment.

articles.title: Bespoke immune cells stave off ravages of cirrhosis
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 29 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01670-xDeath and the need for liver transplant were delayed in people with cirrhosis who received macrophage therapy.
articles.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical advancement where bespoke macrophage therapy delays death and the need for liver transplants in cirrhosis patients, indicating a meaningful health breakthrough with broad impact.
articles.personality_title: Custom immune cells help delay liver transplant in cirrhosis patients
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cirrhosis is a serious liver disease where the liver becomes damaged and scarred over time. This can lead to liver failure, and many patients eventually need a liver transplant to survive.

**What happened** – Scientists have developed a treatment using specially made immune cells called macrophages. These cells were given to people with cirrhosis to help their damaged livers. The treatment slowed down the disease, delaying both death and the need for a liver transplant.

**Impact** – This is important because liver transplants are rare and hard to get. By delaying the need for a transplant, patients can live longer with better health. It also means less pressure on hospitals and transplant programs.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue testing this macrophage therapy to see how well it works for more patients and how long the benefits last. They hope to make this treatment available in hospitals worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new therapy using custom immune cells can slow liver damage and postpone liver transplants in people with cirrhosis.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des cellules immunitaires sur mesure retardent la transplantation hépatique chez les patients atteints de cirrhose
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La cirrhose est une maladie grave du foie où cet organe est endommagé et cicatrisé avec le temps. Cela peut entraîner une insuffisance hépatique, et de nombreux patients ont besoin d’une transplantation du foie pour survivre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé un traitement utilisant des cellules immunitaires spéciales appelées macrophages. Ces cellules ont été données à des personnes atteintes de cirrhose pour aider leur foie endommagé. Le traitement a ralenti la maladie, retardant la mort et la nécessité d’une transplantation.

**Impact** – C’est important car les greffes de foie sont rares et difficiles à obtenir. En retardant la transplantation, les patients peuvent vivre plus longtemps avec une meilleure santé. Cela réduit aussi la pression sur les hôpitaux et les programmes de transplantation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à tester cette thérapie aux macrophages pour voir son efficacité sur plus de patients et la durée des bienfaits. Ils espèrent rendre ce traitement disponible dans les hôpitaux du monde entier.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle thérapie utilisant des cellules immunitaires sur mesure peut ralentir les dommages au foie et retarder les transplantations chez les personnes atteintes de cirrhose.
articles.personality_title_es: Células inmunitarias personalizadas retrasan el trasplante de hígado en pacientes con cirrosis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La cirrosis es una enfermedad grave del hígado donde el órgano se daña y cicatriza con el tiempo. Esto puede causar insuficiencia hepática, y muchos pacientes necesitan un trasplante de hígado para sobrevivir.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un tratamiento con células inmunitarias especiales llamadas macrófagos. Estas células se dieron a personas con cirrosis para ayudar a sus hígados dañados. El tratamiento ralentizó la enfermedad, retrasando la muerte y la necesidad de un trasplante.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque los trasplantes de hígado son raros y difíciles de conseguir. Al retrasar el trasplante, los pacientes pueden vivir más tiempo con mejor salud. También reduce la presión sobre hospitales y programas de trasplantes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán probando esta terapia con macrófagos para ver qué tan bien funciona en más pacientes y cuánto duran los beneficios. Esperan que el tratamiento esté disponible en hospitales de todo el mundo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva terapia con células inmunitarias personalizadas puede frenar el daño hepático y retrasar los trasplantes de hígado en personas con cirrosis.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, intricately shaped shield made of intertwined, vibrant immune cells gently embracing and protecting a stylized, healthy liver form surrounded by soft, natural earth tones and subtle light, symbolizing bespoke immune therapy combating cirrhosis without showing any human figures.

articles.title: Repairing DNA damage: Scientists discover a surprising new benefit of melatonin
articles.summary: A new study suggests melatonin supplements may help night shift workers boost their body's DNA repair processes, potentially offsetting some of the damage linked to working overnight. The findings are early but raise the possibility of a simple strategy to help reduce long-term health risks associated with night shift work.
articles.content:
Melatonin supplements may help the body repair DNA damage linked to night shift work, according to a small clinical trial published in Occupational & Environmental Medicine.

The findings point to a possible way to counter one of the hidden biological effects of working through the night. However, the researchers stress that larger studies are needed before melatonin can be recommended as a long-term strategy for reducing cancer risk in night shift workers.

How Night Shifts Disrupt the Body

Melatonin is best known as the hormone that helps regulate sleep. It rises in darkness and signals to the body that it is time to rest. For people who work overnight, that natural rhythm can be disrupted.

Normal night-time melatonin production is often suppressed in night shift workers. According to the researchers, this may weaken the body's ability to repair oxidative DNA damage, a type of cellular wear and tear that occurs as part of normal metabolism.

That matters because reduced DNA repair may be one pathway linking long-term night shift work with higher risk for certain cancers. Night shift work has also drawn attention from major health agencies because of its effects on the body's internal clock, sleep patterns, light exposure, and hormone signaling.

Testing Melatonin in Night Shift Workers

To explore whether melatonin could improve DNA repair, researchers conducted a randomized placebo controlled trial involving 40 night shift workers.

Half of the participants took a 3 mg melatonin pill once daily for 4 weeks. They took the supplement with food about 1 hour before going to sleep during the day. The other half took a 3 mg placebo pill on the same schedule.

All participants had been working at least two consecutive night shifts each week for at least 6 months. Each shift lasted at least 7 hours. None of the participants had sleep disorders or long-term health conditions.

A Marker of DNA Repair Rose During Daytime Sleep

The researchers collected urine samples during two study periods. One sample period took place before the trial began, and the other occurred near the end of the 4 week intervention. Samples were collected during daytime sleep after night shift work and during the following night shift.

Participants also wore activity trackers so the researchers could measure how long they slept during the day.

The team measured urinary levels of 8-OHdG, a marker used to assess oxidative DNA damage repair capacity. Higher urinary levels during sleep were interpreted as a sign of greater repair activity.

Among workers who took melatonin, urinary 8-OHdG levels were 80% higher during daytime sleep compared with those who took the placebo. That suggests melatonin may have boosted DNA repair while participants were sleeping after night work.

However, the same effect was not seen during the subsequent night shift. During that period, urinary 8-OHdG levels did not differ significantly between the melatonin and placebo groups.

Why the Findings Matter

The study offers a possible explanation for how melatonin might help reduce some of the biological strain caused by working at night. The body normally uses sleep and circadian timing to coordinate repair processes. When people work overnight and sleep during daylight hours, that system may not function as well.

Melatonin may help restore part of that lost signal, at least during daytime sleep. Still, the study was small and short, and it did not measure cancer outcomes. It only measured a biomarker related to DNA repair.

Most participants also worked in healthcare, which means the results may not apply to all night shift workers. The researchers were also unable to account for natural light exposure, which can affect melatonin levels in the body.

Newer Context on Night Shift Work

Since the trial was published in 2025, broader research has continued to highlight the complex ways night shift work may affect health. Recent reviews have pointed to several possible mechanisms, including circadian disruption, altered hormone signaling, changes in immune function, metabolic disruption, and impaired DNA repair.

Major scientific assessments have also treated persistent night shift work and light at night as important public health concerns. The International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified night shift work as probably carcinogenic to humans, and the National Toxicology Program has reviewed evidence linking persistent night shift work and light at night with cancer risk.

These findings do not prove that melatonin supplements prevent cancer. Instead, they strengthen the rationale for studying whether restoring melatonin signaling could help reduce some of the biological effects of long-term night work.

Researchers Urge Caution

The researchers emphasize that their findings should be tested in larger studies involving different doses and longer follow up periods.

They write: "Increased oxidative DNA damage due to diminished DNA repair capacity is a compelling mechanism that may contribute to the carcinogenicity of night shift work. Our randomized placebo-controlled trial suggested melatonin supplementation may improve oxidative DNA damage repair capacity among night shift workers."

And they conclude: "Our findings warrant future larger-scale studies that examine varying doses of melatonin supplements and longer-term impacts of melatonin use. Pending the outcome of such studies, melatonin supplementation may prove to be a viable intervention strategy to reduce the burden of cancer among night shift workers."

They add: "Assessing long-term efficacy is critical since those who work night shifts for many years would need to consistently consume melatonin supplements over that time frame to maximize the potential cancer prevention benefits."

For now, the results suggest that melatonin may do more than support sleep. It may also help night shift workers activate a key repair process while their bodies recover during the day. But whether that translates into meaningful long-term protection remains an open question.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a promising clinical trial finding that melatonin supplements may enhance DNA repair in night shift workers, potentially reducing health risks linked to night work. This represents a positive real-world impact with broad public health significance, especially given the large number of people engaged in night shift work globally. The article provides detailed context about the study design, results, and implications, although it notes the need for further research.
articles.personality_title: Melatonin may help night shift workers repair DNA damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Night shift work can disrupt the body’s natural rhythms, including the production of melatonin, a hormone that helps regulate sleep. This disruption may weaken the body’s ability to fix damage to DNA, which could increase health risks over time.

**What happened** – In a small clinical trial, 40 night shift workers took either melatonin supplements or a placebo for four weeks. Researchers measured a marker in their urine that shows how well the body repairs DNA damage. Those who took melatonin had 80% higher levels of this marker during daytime sleep compared to those who did not.

**Impact** – This study suggests melatonin might help the body repair some of the damage caused by working overnight. While it doesn’t prove melatonin prevents diseases like cancer, it offers a possible way to reduce one of the hidden effects of night shift work.

**What’s next step** – Scientists want to conduct larger and longer studies to see if melatonin supplements can truly lower health risks for night shift workers. They also want to test different doses and understand how melatonin helps the body over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Melatonin supplements may boost the body’s ability to repair DNA damage in night shift workers, pointing to a potential new way to protect their health.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mélatonine pourrait aider les travailleurs de nuit à réparer les dommages à l’ADN
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le travail de nuit perturbe les rythmes naturels du corps, notamment la production de mélatonine, une hormone qui régule le sommeil. Cette perturbation pourrait affaiblir la capacité du corps à réparer les dommages à l’ADN, ce qui peut augmenter les risques pour la santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans un petit essai clinique, 40 travailleurs de nuit ont pris soit des suppléments de mélatonine, soit un placebo pendant quatre semaines. Les chercheurs ont mesuré un marqueur dans leurs urines qui montre la capacité du corps à réparer les dommages à l’ADN. Ceux qui ont pris de la mélatonine avaient 80 % de marqueur en plus pendant leur sommeil de jour.

**Impact** – Cette étude suggère que la mélatonine pourrait aider le corps à réparer certains dommages causés par le travail de nuit. Même si cela ne prouve pas que la mélatonine prévient des maladies comme le cancer, cela ouvre une piste pour réduire un effet caché du travail nocturne.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques souhaitent réaliser des études plus larges et plus longues pour vérifier si la mélatonine peut vraiment diminuer les risques pour la santé des travailleurs de nuit. Ils veulent aussi tester différentes doses et comprendre ses effets sur le long terme.

**Phrase clé** – Les suppléments de mélatonine pourraient renforcer la réparation de l’ADN chez les travailleurs de nuit, offrant une nouvelle piste pour protéger leur santé.
articles.personality_title_es: La melatonina podría ayudar a reparar el daño en el ADN de los trabajadores nocturnos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El trabajo nocturno puede alterar los ritmos naturales del cuerpo, incluyendo la producción de melatonina, una hormona que regula el sueño. Esta alteración podría debilitar la capacidad del cuerpo para reparar daños en el ADN, aumentando riesgos para la salud.

**Qué pasó** – En un pequeño ensayo clínico, 40 trabajadores nocturnos tomaron suplementos de melatonina o un placebo durante cuatro semanas. Los investigadores midieron un marcador en la orina que indica la reparación del daño en el ADN. Quienes tomaron melatonina tuvieron un 80 % más de este marcador durante el sueño diurno.

**Impacto** – El estudio sugiere que la melatonina podría ayudar al cuerpo a reparar algunos daños causados por el trabajo nocturno. Aunque no prueba que la melatonina prevenga enfermedades como el cáncer, ofrece una posible forma de reducir efectos ocultos del trabajo en la noche.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean hacer estudios más grandes y largos para saber si la melatonina puede reducir riesgos de salud en trabajadores nocturnos. También quieren probar diferentes dosis y entender mejor sus efectos a largo plazo.

**Frase clave** – Los suplementos de melatonina podrían mejorar la reparación del ADN en trabajadores nocturnos, abriendo una nueva vía para proteger su salud.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly glowing night sky transitions into gentle daylight over a calm landscape where a luminous, stylized DNA helix is gently embraced by a glowing crescent moon shaped like a pill, symbolizing melatonin supporting DNA repair during daytime rest after night work.

articles.title: DR Congo sees first recovery from Ebola as disease scourges the country
articles.summary: A patient in the DRC has been confirmed to have recovered - a first since the outbreak was detected in mid-May. WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus visited the country on Friday, where authorities are still struggling to contain the spread of the disease. Story by Jennie Shin.
articles.content: A patient in the DRC has been confirmed to have recovered - a first since the outbreak was detected in mid-May. WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus visited the country on Friday, where authorities are still struggling to contain the spread of the disease. Story by Jennie Shin.
articles.source: Health: news, videos, reports and analysis - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports the first recovery from an Ebola outbreak in the DRC, indicating progress in combating a serious public health crisis. This has meaningful positive impact on affected communities and is significant for global health efforts. The article provides clear context about the outbreak timeline and WHO involvement.
articles.personality_title: First Ebola patient recovers in DR Congo amid ongoing outbreak
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since mid-May, the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) has been facing an outbreak of Ebola, a serious and often deadly disease. Health authorities and the World Health Organization (WHO) have been working to control the spread.

**What happened** – A patient in the DRC has fully recovered from Ebola, marking the first confirmed recovery since the outbreak began. WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus visited the country on Friday to support efforts and see the situation firsthand.

**Impact** – This recovery shows that treatment and response efforts can work against Ebola, offering hope to patients and communities affected. It also provides important information for health workers on how to manage the disease and may encourage others to seek help early.

**What's next step** – Health teams in the DRC will continue their work to stop the outbreak, using lessons learned from this case. The WHO will keep supporting local efforts to treat patients and prevent new infections.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The first Ebola recovery in the DRC signals progress in fighting the outbreak and strengthens efforts to protect communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Première guérison d’Ebola en RDC pendant l’épidémie en cours
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis la mi-mai, la République démocratique du Congo (RDC) fait face à une épidémie d’Ebola, une maladie grave et souvent mortelle. Les autorités sanitaires et l’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) travaillent pour contrôler sa propagation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un patient en RDC a complètement guéri d’Ebola, ce qui constitue la première guérison confirmée depuis le début de l’épidémie. Le directeur général de l’OMS, Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, s’est rendu dans le pays vendredi pour soutenir les efforts et voir la situation sur place.

**Impact** – Cette guérison montre que les traitements et les réponses peuvent fonctionner contre Ebola, offrant de l’espoir aux patients et aux communautés touchées. Elle fournit aussi des informations importantes aux soignants sur la gestion de la maladie et peut encourager d’autres personnes à demander de l’aide rapidement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les équipes sanitaires en RDC continueront leur travail pour arrêter l’épidémie, en tirant des leçons de ce cas. L’OMS continuera d’appuyer les efforts locaux pour soigner les malades et prévenir de nouvelles infections.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La première guérison d’Ebola en RDC marque un progrès dans la lutte contre l’épidémie et renforce les efforts pour protéger les populations.
articles.personality_title_es: Primer paciente con Ébola se recupera en RDC durante brote activo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde mediados de mayo, la República Democrática del Congo (RDC) enfrenta un brote de Ébola, una enfermedad grave y a menudo mortal. Las autoridades de salud y la Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) trabajan para controlar su propagación.

**Qué pasó** – Un paciente en la RDC se ha recuperado completamente del Ébola, siendo esta la primera recuperación confirmada desde que comenzó el brote. El director de la OMS, Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, visitó el país el viernes para apoyar los esfuerzos y conocer la situación directamente.

**Impacto** – Esta recuperación demuestra que los tratamientos y las respuestas pueden funcionar contra el Ébola, ofreciendo esperanza a los pacientes y comunidades afectadas. También brinda información importante a los trabajadores de salud sobre cómo manejar la enfermedad y puede motivar a otros a buscar ayuda temprano.

**Próximo paso** – Los equipos de salud en la RDC continuarán trabajando para detener el brote, usando las lecciones aprendidas de este caso. La OMS seguirá apoyando los esfuerzos locales para tratar a los pacientes y prevenir nuevas infecciones.

**Frase resumen** – La primera recuperación de Ébola en la RDC señala un avance en la lucha contra el brote y fortalece los esfuerzos para proteger a las comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a glowing, resilient baobab tree standing strong in the heart of a lush Congolese landscape at dawn, its roots intertwined with a clear, flowing river symbolizing recovery and hope amid a subtle, distant mist representing the lingering presence of Ebola.
